item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The overuse of "$" really killed it for me, I suggest you replace it with something else because it looks too distracting and it takes a lot out of the article's punch.

I don't really mind 3999 being namedropped, just so long as it adds a level of realism and immersion to the article.

That said, I think this needs just a little more time being worked on. I really enjoyed the format screw (despite some parts looking quite messed up and confusing) and I think it's not impossible to bring 3999 in an article; its just plain hard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm of the same mind as some of the other people who posted, but I'd also note that some of the formatting decisions made it hard for me to read (this is possibly specific to me, but I'd imagine it's not.) The lack of spacing between the individual sections of the collapsed documents make them read as one giant paragraph. To illustrate, I think

Interviewed: SCP-3692-1, Dr. Jamie Omensen, 27-year old junior researcher recently diagnosed with [DATA EXPUNGED].
Interviewer: Dr. June Rhodes, Site-██ administrator and senior researcher of SCP-3692.

would read much better as

Interviewed: SCP-3692-1, Dr. Jamie Omensen, 27-year old junior researcher recently diagnosed with [DATA EXPUNGED].

Interviewer: Dr. June Rhodes, Site-██ administrator and senior researcher of SCP-3692.

Also, the kerning of the system font has always made it very easy for my eyes to slide over it, though I'm not sure how much can be done about that here. On a content note, the O5 note at the end feels very out of place, even for an article that plays with the format very heavily. Too much like an outside figure stepping in to tell the reader something for my taste, especially with the return to a normal format at the end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ahh, understandable. That wasn't necessarily directed straight at you alone, by the way. And 3999 is basically interchangeable with 3005, 3211, etc in my above reply - the point being that this is a style I'm using to my own ends. I'd also like to know if you can think of the skip this more closely resembles!

+ Explanation of concept + reply
- Hide this mess

Regarding your criticism about the suspension of disbelief - it's supposed to be on shaky ground to begin with. Not shattered entirely, so for you at least, I failed there. But clearance Levels 2 and 3 have more things separate from them aside from the blue bits and un-redacted info ("this may hurt" -> "this will hurt", the computer's inconsistent replies to invalid commands, and a lot other smaller easter eggs) - which are meant to be hints that something isn't right. Then when June relives Jamie's death, that's when they break.

The overarching concept (well, one layer) of this is that June is writing the whole thing, computer terminal and all, in the notebook, in an attempt to cling to their identity and their reality, which fails as real life and other fictions bleed in. They then fade away when I stop writing them/when they stop writing this article (hence "I need to keep writing"). My biggest issue writing this was putting enough hints into the first two articles to build up to the third, without showing my hand too early. Balancing the build-up/escalation with a marginal appearance of normalcy was complicated, to say the least.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In all honesty, about the only thing this shares with 3999 is the meta aspect and some of the bits in the middle, like the repeated Item number and description. There's another skip that this more closely resembles, but I am blanking on the number. 3005 is definitely a close second. Point is, 3999 is the easy go-to for "this has been done because I can remember one article concretely".

This kind of disjointed meta narrative has been cropping up more and more in series IV, and I feel like there aren't a whole lot of places it can go. Or, those places which it can go to have been gone. So this doesn't add anything to the concept which hasn't already been done, as far as I can tell. That being said, my criticism about the suspension of disbelief in the parts of that article that are supposed to be 'normal' is really my larger reason for downvoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As I'm sensing this is going to be a common theme in criticism of my skip, I feel like I'm obligated to address it.

I'd encourage anyone reading this and having the same thoughts regarding 3999 to reread that scp and my own. 3999's author and myself use a similar style (which, I should note, has been for 100+ years and is kind of the zeitgeist of postmodern literature) to very different end goals. We have fundamentally different stories, different statements to make on. If 3999 is Finnegan's Wake, this is House of Leaves. If 3999 is Gravity's Rainbow or Infinite Jest (closer to Pynchon than Wallace due to 3999's abundance of dreams/stream of consciousness) this is theMystery.Doc, or John Dies at the End. I could go on. Was Danielewski aware of and influenced by James Joyce? It certainly seems that way - Joyce undoubtedly pioneered a style of deconstructionist literature, his influence is vast and cannot be understated. But being the first (not that Joyce was, either) does not give one a monopoly on an idea or structure. 3999 is as influenced, at least apparently (I won't speak to the author's intent, but "the author is dead" anyway), by the authors I list in my scp as I am by them and by it. Furthermore, 3999 and 3692 are saying very different things on fiction, identity, emotion, and other concepts. At least in my interpretation of both.

In light of criticism, however, I may take her down and spend some more time working with her - this might be "finished," but all documents are living - but I still stand by my idea.

So is this "3999 but worse," then? Maybe. Maybe it needs work to more fully stand on its own on this site. Maybe I'll scrap it and write another abstract… thing. That's up for y'all to decide. I just find issue with surface similarities in 1/4th of my article seemingly becoming synecdoche for the deeper meaning of this piece as a whole.

Your commentary is obviously valid - what I'm saying has no more inherent worth as an interpretation than your own. I am curious about the typos you mention, however.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey! I'm glad you liked it - I was more inspired by the likes of House of Leaves and Matthew McIntosh's TheMystery.doc (both are great reads if anyone reading this hasn't checked them out) than 3999 when it came to how I executed my idea, which I'm glad apparently came across.

Thank you for the +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Absolutely go ahead. You've put a finger on the biggest issue I had making this - balancing the format screw with maintaining the illusion that this is mostly "normal" until a point. I have some subtle bits of foreshadowing in the first two iterations, but I'd love any help/new edits you could bring to it. Thank you!!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In my opinion, this does the switch from "basic multi-iteration skip" to "format-screwy monster" way, way too fast. Unlike everybody above, I think there's room for both this and 3999 on the wiki; you just need to execute it much better.

There is a little bit of errant formatting here. Author, do you mind if I comb through this skip and spruce up some of the formatting?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Echoing everyone who's basically said "this is 3999 but worse". Unfortunately 3999 just had beat everyone to this idea, this doesn't really do anything that hasn't already been done.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Far too much about this broke my suspension of disbelief, from the Safe class on the initial document (no way is that not Euclid), to typos, to "$ I'm so sorry, Jamie." (It's worse because she already said she was sorry in the addendum endnote above this.)

When I got to "this is a ripoff of SCP-3999", I'm sorry, the self-awareness doesn't help, and in fact, drawing attention to it made it worse. I wasn't even thinking of 3999 until it was mentioned, and afterward, well, I can't not agree. There are other meta skips that have done this same thing before, and better. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm sorry I didn't get through all of this when you first showed it to me in IRC, but I finished it now!

I feel like it got too personal for me towards the ending. I was kind of uncomfortable, to be honest. Not to say I didn't like it (I did, +1), but it was a bit…much at times?

All-in-all, this is very well written and builds up rather nicely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I definitely understand where you're coming from. 3999 and other similarly-meta or similarly-personal skips are what drew me to this site - they are unabashedly an inspiration, and I could not reasonably hope to make as big a splash as 3999 did when it was first posted. Nor could I claim that this would exist without those articles.

However, I feel like there is room on this site for more experimental literature that delve into higher-concept topics: whether it be the difficulty of the creative process (a la 3999), issues surrounding the idea of the self/identity (like this one), the nature of fiction and its role in society (3211, 3500, and others), the inherent limitations of the Foundation's modus operandi (3005 comes to mind), and others. Just my two cents.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like it occupied a little spot somewhere between 3999 and House of Leaves. It was a slower build than 3999 but had a similar conclusion, but with a "everything's mostly nominal, kind of" ending. I enjoyed it and although it may be similar or derivative of other works, that doesn't make it any less worthy of merit. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 3999 is unique in that attempts to create something similar, even if those attempts acknowledge as much, are never going to work as well. 3999 occupies such a huge amount of space within its niche that there really isn't any room for 3999 vol. 2.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I bit off way more than I could chew but I have a big appetite.

Credit for inspiration/several lines and quotes from the following authors:
Nabokov, Wallace, McIntosh, Nietzsche, Barthes, Borges, Danielewski

Other credit to the Elder Scrolls, Wizards of the Coast, and numerous friends of mine for their stories and influence.

Many thanks to Jazstar for his critique, as well as a few of my friends who aren't on this site, but humored me in this.
